udbhav_h_ram.name: Udbhav H Ram
udbhav_h_ram.summary:
Representing Texas, Udbhav H Ram has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U11 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 20 entries in 10 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Udbhav competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Udbhav adapts to anyone:
  • Ayushram Reddy Keshidi (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Udbhav brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
udbhav_h_ram.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Udbhav H Ram, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Udbhav has 20 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Udbhav saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Udbhav has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Udbhav's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BD U11 — we hope Dhruv Shetty has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Udbhav has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Udbhav has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Udbhav has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Udbhav showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in BS U11, which means Udbhav traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Udbhav entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Udbhav paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Udbhav keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Udbhav has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Udbhav knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Udbhav changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

uddhav_kasarabada.name: Uddhav Kasarabada
uddhav_kasarabada.summary:
Out of California, Uddhav Kasarabada has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, capturing an impressive 3 ORC titles across 2 regional championships. ORCs are the highest level of regular-season competition, drawing the strongest fields in each region. Winning at this level repeatedly marks Uddhav as one of the premier players on the circuit.

A true triple-threat, Uddhav competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 3 doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (8 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • 3 ORC titles.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Uddhav adapts to anyone:
  • Prajwal Avadhani (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (11 total events in BD/U15/U17, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Kriti Vasireddy — a trusted U15/XD partner (7 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Uddhav is still ascending.

Uddhav has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Uddhav has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Uddhav consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 4 titles, Uddhav is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
uddhav_kasarabada.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Uddhav Kasarabada, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 56 career entries in 23 tournaments, Uddhav has 4 titles — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 13 entries where Uddhav drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 56 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Uddhav saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2021 NorCal CRC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Uddhav's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 10 entries — a stretch where Uddhav actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 4 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Uddhav plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 3 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Uddhav can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Uddhav has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Uddhav's texts. The go-to partner is Prajwal Avadhani (11 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Prajwal Avadhani has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Uddhav entered 8 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Uddhav's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Uddhav and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Uddhav has shown up 8 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Uddhav is not just losing locally — Uddhav is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kasarabada family travels cross-country so Uddhav can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Uddhav has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Uddhav showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Uddhav traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Uddhav entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Uddhav paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Uddhav's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 4 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 52 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Uddhav is a player who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. Will Uddhav figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Uddhav's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

umesh_putta.name: Umesh Putta
umesh_putta.summary:
Umesh Putta has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 2 entries in 1 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Umesh competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
umesh_putta.roast:
Step right up and meet Umesh Putta, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Umesh is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf), and so far, the only thing Umesh has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Umesh saga began at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

With only 2 entries on the books, Umesh is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Umesh. We will have more material. We always do.

urav_gupta.name: Urav Gupta
urav_gupta.summary:
Urav Gupta has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 5 entries in 2 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Urav has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Urav competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Urav has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
urav_gupta.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Urav Gupta, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Urav is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Egret Midwest OLC, and so far, the only thing Urav has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Urav saga began at 2025 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Urav plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Urav has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Urav has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Urav showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Egret Midwest OLC in BS U15, which means Urav traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Urav is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Urav wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.
